subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from converting a higher-order ODE into a first-order system, through the eigenvalue method and the full decoupling $A=PDP^{-1}$ with $P$, $P^{-1}$, and $D$ computed explicitly, into the matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$, the fundamental matrix and its link $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, and a complex-eigenvalue capstone that produces a spiraling real solution.

takeaway.title: Unit Key Takeaway
takeaway.body: One idea threads the whole unit: a linear system is easiest in its own eigenbasis. Recast higher-order ODEs as $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$, then \highlight{diagonalize $A=PDP^{-1}$} so the modal substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ \highlight{decouples} the system. The \highlight{matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$} packages every mode into one operator and generalizes scalar $e^{at}$; the \highlight{fundamental matrix} collects the same solutions and reproduces $e^{At}$ at $t=0$. Complex eigenvalues simply trade pure exponentials for spirals.

problems.problem: \strong{(To a system)} Convert $y'' + 5y' + 6y = 0$ into a first-order system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$, and state the companion matrix $A$.
problems.solution:
Let $x_1=y$, $x_2=y'$, so $x_1'=x_2$ and $x_2'=y''=-6y-5y'=-6x_1-5x_2$:
\[ \work{\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
The coefficients enter the bottom row with flipped signs.

problems.problem: \strong{(Eigenvalues)} For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $\det(A-\lambda I)$ and find both eigenvalues.
problems.solution:
Expand the determinant:
\[ \work{\det(A-\lambda I) = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (2)(3) = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = (\lambda-4)(\lambda+1).} \]
So $\lambda_1=4$ and $\lambda_2=-1$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Eigenvectors and $P$)} For that same $A$, find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue and assemble the modal matrix $P=[\mathbf{v}_1\mid\mathbf{v}_2]$.
problems.solution:
Solve $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for each eigenvalue:
\[ \work{\lambda=4:\ \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1=(2,3);} \]
\[ \work{\lambda=-1:\ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1).} \]
Hence $P=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

The determinant and inverse are
\[ \work{\det P = (2)(-1)-(1)(3) = -5, \qquad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.} \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Verify decoupling)} Confirm $P^{-1}AP=D$ for the $A$, $P$, $P^{-1}$ above, and state the decoupled system $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$.
problems.solution:
Multiply in two steps, $AP$ then $P^{-1}(AP)$:
\[ \work{AP = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad P^{-1}AP = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = D.} \]
The decoupled system is $y_1'=4y_1$, $y_2'=-y_2$.

problems.problem: \strong{(General solution)} Write the general solution $\mathbf{x}(t)$ of $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ from the eigenpairs $\lambda_1=4,\ \mathbf{v}_1=(2,3)$ and $\lambda_2=-1,\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1)$.
problems.solution:
Each eigenpair contributes one exponential mode:
\[ \work{\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{4t}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
With $\lambda_1=4>0>\lambda_2=-1$ the origin is a saddle.

problems.problem: \strong{(Matrix exponential)} Using $A=PDP^{-1}$ with $D=\operatorname{diag}(4,-1)$, build $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ in closed form.
problems.solution:
Exponentiate the diagonal and reassemble $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$:
\[ \work{Pe^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \quad e^{At} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t}+3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t}-2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t}-3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t}+2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
At $t=0$ this is $I$, as it must be.

problems.problem: \strong{(Fundamental matrix)} Write a fundamental matrix $\Psi(t)$ for this system and compute its Wronskian $\det\Psi(t)$.
problems.solution:
Stack the two eigensolutions as columns:
\[ \work{\Psi(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \det\Psi = -2e^{3t}-3e^{3t} = -5e^{3t} \neq 0.} \]
The nonzero Wronskian confirms the columns are independent.

problems.problem: \strong{(Normalization)} Explain why $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, and identify $\Psi(0)$ for the $\Psi$ above.
problems.solution:
Both $\Psi(t)$ and $e^{At}$ solve $X'=AX$; they agree once normalized to the same value at $t=0$. Since $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$,
\[ \work{e^{At} = \Psi(t)\,\Psi(0)^{-1}, \qquad \Psi(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = P.} \]
Right-multiplying by $\Psi(0)^{-1}$ forces the value $I$ at $t=0$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Complex capstone)} Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, giving the real general solution and the spiral direction.
problems.solution:
Here $\det(A-\lambda I)=\lambda^2-4\lambda+13=0$ gives $\lambda=2\pm 3i$. With $\mathbf{v}=(1,-i)$ for $\lambda=2+3i$, Euler's formula yields
\[ \work{\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{2t}\left[ c_1\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix} + c_2\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix} \right].} \]
Since $\alpha=2>0$, every trajectory spirals outward.
